text: The surgeon visually identifies the seminal vesicles and vas deferens. The Foley catheter balloon is deflated, and an incision is made higher up, following the principles of the nerve-sparing technique.
The surgeon adjusts the cautery setting to two on the XI robotic system, with the bipolar setting also at two. An air seal device maintains the pneumoperitoneum at twelve mmHg during the procedure.
The assistant pushes down the peritoneum while the surgeon locates the posterior layer, visibly lifting the bladder upward during the procedure.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection, lifting the bladder or seminal vesicle from the rectum to reveal a clear anatomical plane. The vas deferens is visible, and the ureter travels along this plane. The surgeon identifies the trigon and considers dissecting one plane deeper.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens on the right side, discussing the incision placement relative to the prostate and bladder, with considerations for using stay sutures.
The surgeon uses an escalab instrument to clip the right vas deferens and begins dissecting the seminal vesicles. The posterior denonvilliers fascia is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a suction device to assist in the dissection of the posterior layer, aiming to reach as far down as possible for easier access. The prostate being operated on is a 47-gram prostate with left-sided prostate cancer. The surgeon prepares to dissect the seminal vesicle.

The surgeon visually identifies the seminal vesicle and begins to decompress it by puncturing, aiding in the dissection of its tip. The seminal vesicle appears dilated and engorged, which can complicate the dissection process.

The surgeon is positioned on top of the neurovascular bundle on the right side, carefully dissecting around the Daniel Biller's fascia. The prostate capsule is visible, and due to the presence of cancer on the left side, a wider dissection is planned on both the left and right sides.
The surgeon begins the interfacial technique on the right side, preparing to dissect more extensively on the left side due to positive findings from MRI imaging.

The surgeon performs a limited dissection on the left side, focusing on the vas deferens and seminal vesicles. The right side dissection will be addressed later. The surgeon clips the structures, ensuring nerve preservation for optimal outcomes.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the vas deferens and proceeds to dissect the seminal vesicle on the left side. The seminal vesicle is decompressed slightly, and small arterioles at the tip of the seminal vesicle are identified and clipped.
The surgeon clips two arteries at the tip of the left seminal vesicle after decompression, minimizing counter traction on the structure.
The surgeon uses forceps to manipulate tissue in the denouement fascia area, with the rectal wall visible below. The surgeon, who is right-handed, prepares to work on the right side first.

The surgeon opens veins around the prostate, with the neurovascular bundle partially addressed. The surgeon proceeds laterally, lifting the bladder away from the prostate using suction to improve visibility and access.
The surgeon lifts the bladder away from the prostate, exposing the junction where the bladder attaches to the prostate. A clear dissection plane is visible, and the apical area is approached.
The surgeon carefully performs interfacial dissection on the right side, avoiding damage to the levator muscles. The neurovascular bundles are visible on the lateral side.

The surgeon mobilizes the right side, attempting to find the plane between the prostate and bladder. A small nodule is visible on the bladder near the prostate, but the surgeon decides not to transect until the left side is addressed.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to manipulate the bladder, ensuring it does not obstruct the surgical field. An incision is made higher up on the left side of the prostate to facilitate better access.

The surgeon is dissecting a part of the neurovascular bundle, making wide incisions to prevent the bladder from falling back into the surgical field. The dissection is performed gently to ensure precision.

The surgeon performs an extra fascial dissection on the bladder neck side, avoiding the apical side, while carefully navigating around the dorsal venous complexes and preserving the levator muscles. The dissection aims to maximize nerve preservation.

The surgeon clips the neurovascular bundle, which is approximately 1.5 centimeters in length, using robotic instruments. The procedure involves careful manipulation to ensure proper placement of the clips.

The surgeon uses robotic forceps to re-grab the area near the neurovascular bundle on the left side, applying metal clips to secure the anterior side without dissecting the posterior side. The neurovascular bundle dissection is nearly complete, and the surgeon lifts the prostate to expose its lateral portions, proceeding cautiously.

The surgeon grasps the bladder on the left side, identifying the correct margin while pushing the bladder away from the prostate. The neurovascular bundle is visible, and the surgeon avoids using cautery to maintain natural tissue planes. The deflated Foley catheter remains inside the bladder during this lateral dissection.

The surgeon uses forceps to apply counter traction, revealing the urethra in the apical area of the prostate. The urethra is clearly visible as the surgeon manipulates the tissue to enhance its exposure.

The surgeon focuses on the dorsal vein area near the urethra, with the Foley catheter still in place. They minimize dissection around the urethra while applying counter traction to reduce surgical trauma and preserve continence. The surgeon attempts to appreciate the dorsal vein and suggests that dissecting the bladder neck completely may improve the view of the dorsal vein complex.

The surgeon uses a lateral approach to cut the urethra, following the technique learned from Dr. Gaston. The Foley catheter is manipulated, being pulled out and then slightly repositioned during the procedure.
The surgeon has exposed the bladder and the left side of the prostate, following anatomical planes for dissection.
The surgeon identifies a small nodule within the surgical field and prepares to excise it using precise cutting techniques.
The surgeon inflates a balloon in the surgical area, applying gentle pressure to compress the dorsal veins. The balloon technique is used to manage tension and blood flow in the region.

The surgeon prepares to perform the anastomosis, changing the needle driver to the left hand and switching the scissor to the pro grasp instrument. The pro grasp is used to lift the bladder at a specific angle, facilitating the procedure. The surgeon opts to use a zero-degree lens for visualization until the end of the procedure.
The surgeon requests a needle and proceeds to push in the Foley catheter, followed by deflation. Perineal pressure is applied as the surgeon prepares to suture.

The surgeon applies figure-of-eight stitches on the dorsal vein complex to control bleeding, using a zero-degree lens for visualization. The stitches are placed carefully to obliterate potential heavy bleeders within the vein complex.

The surgeon identifies the twelve o'clock region of the bladder, marking it with a stitch to clarify its position. The trigon is visible above this area, and the surgeon prepares to suture the twelve o'clock bladder region.

The surgeon sutures the bladder at the twelve o'clock position, incorporating fascia to prevent tearing. An eleven o'clock bladder suture is placed using a larger needle with a V-lock, facilitating the anastomosis. After completing two throws at twelve o'clock and one o'clock, the bladder is cinched down, and the remaining sutures are placed in a circular pattern.
Perineal pressure is applied while the surgeon sutures the bladder at the two o'clock and three o'clock positions. The bladder neck is visible, and sutures are spaced wider to accommodate its size.
The surgeon places a suture at the four-thirty position on the bladder neck, then switches to another needle to continue the anastomosis at the eleven o'clock position.
The surgeon places a suture at the eleven o'clock position on the bladder neck, followed by another suture at the ten o'clock position. The Foley catheter is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to place a nine o'clock stitch, maintaining steady perineal pressure throughout the procedure.
The surgeon performs an inside-out stitch on the urethra, locking the suture to facilitate the anastomosis. The Foley catheter is positioned without inflating the balloon.

The surgeon cinches down the bladder, carefully throwing a few stitches to complete the anastomosis while ensuring the Foley catheter is not caught in the sutures. The catheter is gently moved to avoid interference.
The surgeon replaces the existing catheter with a new one equipped with a 30 cc balloon, adjusting it to 20 cc. Needle drivers are used to assist in the removal process.

The surgeon uses needle drivers to handle and tie off a suture, emphasizing caution with the needle. The Foley catheter is pulled and irrigation is performed to ensure the anastomosis is intact. The air seal system is noted to be functioning well.

The surgeon requests a needle and confirms the removal of instruments from the surgical field. Irrigation is performed, and the anesthesia team is asked to hydrate the patient and administer ten milligrams of Lasix to ensure urine output.
The surgeon uses forceps to perform a sampling biopsy, focusing on the nerve anatomy visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses forceps to carefully pack the surgical area with a piece of Surgicel, cutting it in half to fit the space appropriately.
